reforms.description: The UK government plans to introduce a graduate contribution scheme allowing students to defer tuition fees until after graduation, addressing financial barriers to higher education. This scheme aims to resolve credit constraints for those from poorer backgrounds.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: By improving access to higher education, the reform is expected to enhance the skills and employability of graduates, contributing positively to economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The reform has been officially announced but is not yet implemented.
reforms.importance_rationale: The introduction of a graduate contribution scheme represents a significant structural change in funding higher education.
reforms.source_quote: The plan for a graduate contribution scheme launched in a recent white paper on 'The future of higher education' will deal with this problem by allowing all students to defer the payment of all fees until they have graduated.

reforms.description: The UK government began the phased introduction of activity-based funding for hospitals in April 2003, which allocates funding based on the activity levels of hospitals rather than historic costs.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: This reform is expected to improve efficiency in hospital funding, leading to better resource allocation and potentially enhancing healthcare outcomes.
reforms.status_evidence: The reform has been fully implemented since April 2003.
reforms.importance_rationale: The introduction of activity-based funding represents a major overhaul of the funding mechanism for hospitals.
reforms.source_quote: The NHS began the phased introduction of activity-based funding for hospitals in April 2003.

reforms.description: The UK government initiated a comprehensive framework for reform aimed at helping individuals on incapacity benefits return to work, focusing on addressing barriers such as health, financial issues, and lack of skills. Additionally, a Skills Strategy was presented to enhance training responsiveness to employer needs, allowing firms more control over publicly-funded training.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: By improving skills and training responsiveness, the reform aims to enhance employability and productivity in the workforce.
reforms.status_evidence: Both components of the reform have been implemented since 2003.
reforms.importance_rationale: The reforms represent significant but narrower changes aimed at improving skills and employment outcomes.
reforms.source_quote: In July 2003, the UK Government presented a Skills Strategy to make available training more responsive to the specific skill needs of employers and workers.

reforms.description: The Department of Trade and Industry announced a significant simplification and reduction in the number of Business Support schemes, consolidating them into ten distinct products to enhance effectiveness in promoting innovation and entrepreneurship.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: By streamlining business support, the reform aims to improve access to resources for innovation and entrepreneurship, potentially boosting economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The reform has been officially announced but is not yet implemented.
reforms.importance_rationale: The consolidation of Business Support schemes represents a major reform in the governance of innovation support.
reforms.source_quote: In a welcome move the Department of Trade and Industry has announced a massive simplification and reduction in the number of Business Support schemes which will be consolidated into ten 'products'.
